entries.title: Asha Bhosle, one of India's most versatile Bollywood singers, dies at 92
entries.summary: <p>NEW DELHI — Asha Bhosle, one of India's most versatile Bollywood singers whose performances shaped the country's musical memory and modern cinema, has died. She was 92.</p><p>The legendary singer across genres died Sunday of multiple organ failure at Breach Candy Hospital in Mumbai, Pratit Samdani, a physician at the hospital said. Her son, Anand Bhosle, told reporters that her last rites will be performed on Monday.</p><p>Asha was admitted at the hospital on late Saturday with a chest infection and exhaustion, her granddaughter Zanai Bhosle said in a social media post.</p><p>Asha's timeless voice resonated across a film-obsessed India for nearly eight decades, recorded on about 12,000 songs. She boldly embraced cabaret and Western-influenced melodies to forge a distinct musical identity that stood uniquely apart from her sister, Lata Mangeshkar, herself a legendary voice revered as the "Melody Queen."</p><p>Asha's death was widely condoled.</p><p>"I am deeply saddened" by her passing, Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi said in a social media post.</p><p>"Her unique musical journey spanning decades has enriched our cultural heritage and touched the hearts of countless people around the world," Modi said. "From soulful melodies to spirited compositions, her voice carried a timeless brilliance."</p><p>Born on Sept. 8, 1933, Asha Bhosle was initiated into music by her father Dinanath Mangeshkar, who was also a trained singer. All her four siblings became accomplished singers and musicians.</p><p>Asha's first marriage, in 1949, ended in separation in 1960. Her second marriage was to iconic music composer R.D. Burman in 1980. She is survived by a son and grandchildren.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Trump lambasts Pope Leo XIV, extending feud over Iran war with first American pontiff
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump delivered an extraordinary broadside against Pope Leo XIV on Sunday night, saying he didn't think the U.S.-born global leader of the Catholic Church is "doing a very good job" and that "he's a very liberal person," while also suggesting the pontiff should "stop catering to the Radical Left."</p><p>Flying back to Washington from Florida, Trump used a lengthy social media post to sharply criticize Leo, then kept it up after deplaning, in comments on the tarmac to reporters.</p><p>"I'm not a fan of Pope Leo," he said.</p><p>Trump's comments came after Leo suggested over the weekend that a "delusion of omnipotence" is fueling the U.S.-Israel war in Iran. While it's not unusual for popes and presidents to be at cross purposes, it's exceedingly rare for the pope to directly criticize a U.S. leader — and Trump's stinging response is equally uncommon, if not more so.</p><p>"Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy," the president wrote in his post, adding, "I don't want a Pope who thinks it's OK for Iran to have a Nuclear Weapon."</p><p>He repeated that sentiment in comments to reporters, saying, "We don't like a pope who says it's OK to have a nuclear weapon."</p><p>Later, Trump posted a picture suggesting he had saint-like powers akin to those of Jesus Christ. Wearing a biblical-style robe, Trump is seen laying hands on a bedridden man as light emanates from his fingers, while a soldier, a nurse, a praying woman and a bearded man in a baseball cap all look on admiringly. The sky above is filled with eagles, an American flag and vaporous images.</p><p>All of that came after Leo presided over an evening prayer service in St. Peter's Basilica on Saturday, the same day the United States and Iran began face-to-face negotiations in Pakistan during a fragile ceasefire. The pope didn't mention the United States or Trump by name, but his tone and message appeared directed at Trump and U.S. officials, who have boasted of U.S. military superiority and justified the war in religious terms.</p><p>Leo — who is scheduled to leave Monday for an 11-day trip to Africa — has previously said that God "does not listen to the prayers of those who wage war, but rejects them." He's also referenced an Old Testament passage from Isaiah, saying that "even though you make many prayers, I will not listen — your hands are full of blood."</p><p>Before the ceasefire, when Trump warned of mass strikes against Iranian power plants and other infrastructure and that "an entire civilization will die tonight," Leo described such sentiments as "truly unacceptable."</p><p>In his social media post on Sunday night, however, Trump went far beyond the war in Iran in criticizing Leo.</p><p>The president wrote, "I don't want a Pope who thinks it's terrible that America attacked Venezuela, a Country that was sending massive amounts of Drugs into the United States." That was a reference to the Trump administration having ousted Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro in January.</p><p>"I don't want a Pope who criticizes the President of the United States because I'm doing exactly what I was elected, IN A LANDSLIDE, to do," Trump added, referencing his 2024 election victory.</p><p>He also suggested in the post that Leo only got his position "because he was an American, and they thought that would be the best way to deal with President Donald J. Trump."</p><p>"If I wasn't in the White House, Leo wouldn't be in the Vatican," Trump wrote, adding, "Leo should get his act together as Pope, use Common Sense, stop catering to the Radical Left, and focus on being a Great Pope, not a Politician. It's hurting him very badly and, more importantly, it's hurting the Catholic Church!"</p><p>In his subsequent comments to reporters, Trump remained highly critical, saying of Leo, "I don't think he's doing a very good job. He likes crime I guess" and adding, "He's a very liberal person."</p><p>In the 2024 election, Trump won 55% of Catholic voters, according to AP VoteCast, an extensive survey of the electorate. But Trump's administration also has close ties to conservative evangelical Protestant leaders and has claimed heavenly endorsement for the war on Iran.</p><p>Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth urged Americans to pray for victory "in the name of Jesus Christ." And, when Trump was asked whether he thought God approved of the war, he said, "I do, because God is good — because God is good and God wants to see people taken care of."<br></p>
entries.author: Josh Boak | AP

entries.title: 3 hospitalized after shooting in New City
entries.summary: <p>Three men were shot in Chicago’s New City neighborhood on Sunday night, according to police.</p><p>Before 8 p.m. in the 5400 block of South Laflin Street, three men were near the sidewalk when an unknown car approached them, police said. Two people in the car pulled out handguns and fired shots at the men.</p><p>A 29-year-old man was struck in the hand and taken to St. Bernard Hospital, where he was reported in fair condition. Another man, 30, was struck in both legs and taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center in fair condition. A 31-year-old man was struck in the arm and taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center in fair condition.</p><p>Area detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 'The Decision' is coming for Billy Donovan and future with the Bulls
entries.summary: <p>DALLAS — There will be no Boys & Girls Club anywhere in sight.</p><p>Jim Gray, LeBron James? Stay at home.</p><p>This “decision” is Bulls coach Billy Donovan’s to make and his to make alone. He made that clear Sunday with the regular season ending in the 149-128 loss to Dallas and his future up in the air.</p><p>He did divulge that he plans to sit down with owners Jerry and Michael Reinsdorf early this week, doesn’t plan to drag his decision on and will make it by himself.</p><p>There was a lot of speculation that with Donovan’s father and mother-in-law dying in the same week in February and his mother dealing with health issues there would be a lengthy discussion with family members, but it doesn’t work that way for the Hall of Famer.</p><p>“One is, I feel like I need to [make a quick decision] moving forward and certainly listen to what they have to say,” Donovan said. “The one thing, as difficult [a time] as I went through at the All-Star break, and I mean this in all respect, my family is not going to necessarily have anything to do with the decision. They have been incredibly supportive, and it would be more my internal [decision], with my mom and her situation, that would be something that I would want to do.</p><p>“It wouldn’t be a situation where I’m feeling a certain family pressure of, ‘Hey, listen, can you go take some time? We’ve gone through some of these difficulties.’ They’ve never put me in that situation. I’m grateful for that. So if it would have anything to do with the family, it would totally be on me.”</p><p>Donovan also ruled out the idea that he would survey the landscape of potential job openings and shop his services around.</p><p>He almost made it sound like if he does decide to walk away, there would be some downtime — maybe taking a season off — to reassess what he wants from the profession.</p><p>“No, my commitment and loyalty is to the Bulls,” he said when asked if he would play the wait-and-see game for another NBA gig to open.</p><p>The Sun-Times did learn that it sounds as if Donovan is committed to staying in the NBA, with or without the Bulls.</p><p>As for the Bulls, the Sun-Times reported that the one job they were concerned about that might entice Donovan right away would be Orlando’s if the Magic move off Jamahl Mosley.</p><p>In the wake of the firings of executive vice president Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley, Michael Reinsdorf made it clear that keeping Donovan was a top priority, and they want him to have more input in the decision-making.</p><p>That’s all well and good, but the most important decision Donovan will be making soon is whether he wants to be a part of what feels like a rebuild. The loss did allow the Bulls (31-51) to lock up the No. 9 lottery draft spot.</p><p>“Obviously, things have changed with Arturas and Marc, and you go through the season thinking we’re all kind of going to go through [an exit meeting] together,” Donovan said. “That’s changed, and I’m sure [ownership wants] to talk about what the plan is going forward, what their thoughts are, all those kinds of things. We’ve always done that after every year of how we can get better and those types of things.</p><p>“I love being in Chicago and I love the organization, everything about it, but we’re in this pivot point right now where everything has changed. So for me to sit down and have a conversation, that’s really what I want to do, just to find out where we’re all at with this and how we’re going to move forward.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Two teams, two timelines, two different bets on Angel Reese
entries.summary: <p>The Sky and the Dream have two different views on Angel Reese. This is the kind of thing that prompts a trade.</p><p>The Dream think Reese could help them contend for a championship right now.</p><p>The Sky, whose championship window is still years off, decided future draft picks were more valuable — and were willing to part ways with her.</p><p>General manager Jeff Pagliocca hasn’t spoken publicly about why the Sky’s valuation of Reese changed, from trading up to draft her in 2024 to shipping her away after two All-Star seasons. His statement after the deal said the trade was designed to achieve ‘‘roster balance.’’</p><p>The last couple of seasons, however, have offered clearer clues.</p><p>First there was the basketball element. Pairing Reese and Kamilla Cardoso in the frontcourt never totally clicked, especially now that coach Tyler Marsh wants to run a pace-and-space offense.</p><p>Then there was the locker-room element — more specifically, the interview-heard-round-the-world.</p><p>Near the end of last season, Reese went public with frustrations about the Sky’s direction and the competitiveness of the roster. The comments <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/09/05/sky-caught-off-guard-by-angel-reeses-interview" target="_blank" >bothered her teammates</a>, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/09/07/whose-idea-was-it-to-suspend-angel-reese" target="_blank" >ownership suspended her for a half-game</a> even after she apologized.</p><p>Since then, multiple team sources say key executives — including principal owner Michael Alter — were concerned about whether Reese fit the Sky’s culture. The Dream aren’t concerned about any of that.</p><p>At a news conference Sunday, Dream GM Dan Padover and coach Karl Smesko were asked directly whether they worried about Reese’s effect on the team’s culture. Did the interview-heard-round-the-world give them pause? Not a bit, they said.</p><p>‘‘We always look forward,’’ Padover said. ‘‘To get an opportunity to bring a player of Angel’s talent in and someone that our players deeply respect — for us, it was a no-brainer.’’</p><p>In fact, the Dream have had their eye on Reese throughout their rise to contention.</p><p>‘‘Angel — that was something at the end of last year Dan and I spoke about,’’ Smesko said. ‘‘We really thought that would be a great addition for us if we could do something like that.</p><p>‘‘Angel’s a special talent. Everybody’s aware that she’s the best rebounder in the league. I also appreciate the other skills that she brings to the team, from competitiveness to being able to handle the ball. She’s somebody that can grab and go. She can be on either side of a pick-and-roll. I think that versatility just makes her a unique talent.’’</p><p>Funny thing is, six months ago, those words could have come straight from a Sky news conference.</p><p>In his first season as head coach, Marsh loved optimizing Reese’s versatility, using her as a playmaker rather than a low-post threat. And Pagliocca used to rave about her competitiveness and toughness.</p><p>Will the two of them end up wishing they had given Reese more time? Will the Dream, already built to contend, be just what she needed?</p><p>Time will tell.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Bulls forward Isaac Okoro enters another offseason of uncertainty
entries.summary: <p>DALLAS – There’s a business of basketball that Isaac Okoro has become very accustomed to.</p><p>In his previous few seasons in Cleveland, the forward was seemingly a trade rumor several times a calendar year. February, his name was typically floating around at the deadline, and then of course during the summer months.</p><p>That was until rumor became reality for him last July, and the Bulls acquired him in the Lonzo Ball trade with the Cavaliers.</p><p>So with the regular season officially ending on Sunday for the veteran and the lottery-bound Bulls, it would seem like Okoro could finally put his feet up the next few months, focus on his game, knowing that he still has one year left on his current deal and the skills he brings to the team are highly regarded.</p><p>Think again.</p><p>With so much unknown around the franchise – including who will run the front office and if coach Billy Donovan will even stick around – Okoro knows he needs to be prepared for anything and going anywhere.</p><p>“Yeah, I think I’ll just control what I can control,” Okoro said. “I’ll go into the offseason and try to figure out how I can be a better player for next season. It’s the NBA, it’s the business, and I can be traded again somewhere. I love being here and I love the culture we’re trying to build, so I just have to try and control what I can control.</p><p>“But I’ve gotten used to it. I’ve only been traded one time, but in Cleveland come every offseason I felt like I could have been traded.”</p><p>Whoever takes over the front office duties will have a lot to digest with Okoro. He came over as almost a finishing piece to a roster that lacked a true wing defender and physicality. He did lead the Bulls in drawing charges with 14 (Coby White led them last season with three), but there was only so much he could do on the defensive end, especially when the roster was flipped at the trade deadline and all the new faces led to a lot of confusion night in and night out on that end of the floor.</p><p>Would he be better served as a finishing piece on a contender rather than a rebuild? Obviously, but do the Bulls want to keep him around to also help with the culture in doing the ugly things that need to be done?</p><p>That’s the wait-and-see.</p><p>“It was definitely a unique season, my most unique season yet,” Okoro said. “But it’s the NBA; you’re going to have guys that go down to injury, players moved, and that can’t be an excuse.</p><p>“There were games we should have won and didn’t. You can’t go back and think about, ‘Oh, we should have done this.’ You just got to be better.”</p><p></p><p><b>Summer fun</b></p><p></p><p>Noa Essengue has had a very uneventful rookie campaign, injuring his shoulder and having just six minutes on his NBA resume.</p><p>With season-ending surgery behind him, however, he’s hoping the business of basketball is about to pick up, cleared to start playing one-on-one and two-on-two in the next couple weeks. The goal is to get him up and running, ready for full contact and Summer League play by early July.</p><p>“I think it’s helped me to just understand the game,” Essengue said Sunday of all the down time. “When you’re on the bench you see a lot more than when you’re on the court.”</p><p></p><p><b>Sitting it out</b></p><p></p><p>The ankle injury suffered by Matas Buzelis late in the Orlando game did in fact keep him from finishing off his second year by playing the finale, but he still led the Bulls in games played this season with 77 and was third in average minutes played with 29.2 per game.</p><p></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Tanner Murray’s first career home run helps White Sox salvage series split with Royals
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> finally found some offense Sunday with their first home runs in a week to survive an all-hands-on-deck afternoon for the pitching staff and beat the Royals 6-5 to salvage a four-game series split in Kansas City.</p><p>Following a three-hour rain delay, <b>Tanner Murray</b>’s first career home run in the second inning ended a 21-inning scoring drought for the Sox, who were shut out the previous two games. The rookie’s two-run blast plated <b>Colson Montgomery</b>, who had doubled off Royals starter <b>Noah Cameron</b> and later mashed a two-run bomb of his own.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Tanner Murray making his first career home run count! <a href="https://t.co/3TVDPXKkIA">pic.twitter.com/3TVDPXKkIA</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2043441921398338047?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>After<b> Grant Taylor</b>’s flawless two-inning <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>opening appearance</u></a>, <b>Sean Newcomb</b> followed in the third inning and soon gave way to <b>Jonathan Cannon</b>, who looked off from the start.</p><p>Cannon’s first pitch since being called up from Charlotte was wild, allowing the two baserunners he inherited to advance, before walking one batter to load the bases and two more to tie the game. He exited the game with hip irritation, and manager <b>Will Venable</b> had to call every name in his bullpen as the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/10/anthony-kay-jac-caglianone-white-sox-royals-hit-by-pitch?55" target="_blank" ><u>division rivals</u></a> swapped leads four times.</p><p>"It was incredible," Venable said. "It was not necessarily how you drew it up, but in those types of situations, you need the guys to step up and pitch well, and they did."</p><p>Sox outfielder <b>Dustin Harris</b> opened the seventh with a pinch-hit double off the right-field wall and eventually scored to put the Sox ahead on a bases-loaded wild pitch from KC reliever <b>John Schreiber</b>. Harris later went airborne to rob at least extra bases from Royals second baseman <b>Michael Massey</b>, a Palos Park resident, whose eighth-inning drive fell just a few feet short of a game-tying homer.</p><p>Montgomery was in visible pain after taking a 95-mph sinker off the forearm in the eighth, but he stayed in the game.</p><p>His wrist was "sore but not broke," and the win "felt great," Montgomery said. "The last couple of days we’ve been right there."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Stay fair. It will! <a href="https://t.co/4k0k11jsQ7">pic.twitter.com/4k0k11jsQ7</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2043452090341527759?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Mr. Smith goes to Charlotte</b></h3><p><b>Shane Smith</b> had an uneven outing Sunday in his first start at Triple-A Charlotte after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/04/08/white-sox-option-opening-day-starter-shane-smith-to-triple-a-charlotte" target="_blank" ><u>being demoted</u></a>.</p><p>The Sox’ Opening Day starter and 2025 All-Star gave up three first-inning runs on three hits, a walk and a hit batter, but settled in for three innings after that with five strikeouts.</p><p>The Sox shipped Smith east to straighten things out after he gave up 10 earned runs over three short starts. The rest of the rotation has mostly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/11/davis-martin-white-sox-strong-start" target="_blank" ><u>looked solid</u></a> since a brutal opening week.</p><p>In his first game of a rehabilitation stint with the Knights, outfielder <b>Everson Pereira</b> smacked a 405-foot home run.</p><h3><b>Rebuild remnants</b></h3><p>One-time Sox rebuilding centerpiece <b>Eloy Jimenez</b> was called up Sunday by the Blue Jays for his first big-league action since 2024. He went 2-for-4 with two singles and two strikeouts as Toronto’s DH.</p><p><b>Luis Robert Jr.</b> has hit .319/.458/.447 with two homers, seven RBI and 12 walks over his first 14 games with the Mets. <b>Luisangel Acuna</b>, the Sox’ main return for Robert, is slashing .196/.224/.196 with four stolen bases.</p><p><b>Yoan Moncada</b> (.125/.340/.250 with a homer and four RBI) hasn’t fared much better for the Angels.</p><p>The Sox face the Angels at home April 27-29 and in Anaheim May 4-6. They visit Toronto July 17-19 and welcome the Mets to Rate Field Aug. 21-23.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, April 13, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions from 2 to 4:30 a.m. Chicago time. After that, the moon moves from Aquarius into Pisces.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Your ruler, fiery Mars, is lined up with fuzzy Neptune today, which could make you feel irritable or discouraged. You might doubt yourself, which is not your style. Meanwhile, you might attract money to you or spend money on beautiful things. Don't give away the farm.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today is a mixed bag. It's an excellent day to shop for wardrobe goodies for yourself. However, don't go overboard because of feelings of inadequacy or a desire to overcompensate. You don't have to prove anything to anyone. Tread carefully today.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're high-viz today, which means some people know personal details about your private life. Don't push too hard with issues related to a friend or a group because something is confusing here. (You don't want later regrets.) Meanwhile, a secret romance might be exciting!</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Your dealings with a friend or a group are excellent today. (A casual friendship could turn romantic.) However, you might feel confused about your long-term direction. Be cautious when talking to bosses and authority figures. You might be unsure or discouraged about how to tackle something or deal with someone. If this is the case, don't act today. Don't volunteer for anything. However, if shopping or negotiating with others, be careful because you might not have all the facts. You might feel reluctant to assert yourself and protect your own best interests.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You have the advantage today because the moon is in your sign; however, financial matters might be confusing. If you suspect something fishy is going on, it is. Don’t act if you’re not sure. Nevertheless, communications with others are warm and friendly.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Edward Fox (1937) shares your birthday. You are intellectual, realistic and strong-willed. You are a private person who is curious, methodical and progressive. Take inventory of your life this year. It's time to let go of the people, places and things from the past that held you back. Do some internal and external housecleaning.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Woman fatally struck by vehicle near United Center
entries.summary: <p>A 43-year-old woman was killed after a vehicle struck her on Sunday night on the Near West Side, police said.</p><p>Around 9 p.m., police responded to a car accident in the 10-100 Block of South Ashland Avenue. A 17-year-old female driver told police she was traveling eastbound on Ogden Avenue in a silver SUV when she hit the 43-year-old woman crossing the street against the signal. Police said the woman sustained head trauma and was pronounced dead at Stroger Hospital.</p><p>A witness told police that the teenage driver had the right of way because of a green light, police said.</p><p>No citations are pending and Area Three Detectives are investigating.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 10-year-old girl killed, man critically hurt in Washington Heights fire
entries.summary: <p>A 10-year-old girl was killed in a fire that also left an 18-year-old man critically injured early Monday in Washington Heights on the Far South Side. </p><p>Chicago police and fire officials responded to a residential fire in the 10400 block of South Green Street just before 3:30 a.m., police said. </p><p>The girl was taken to Roseland Hospital where she was pronounced dead, officials said. </p><p>An 18-year-old man was also found inside the home and was taken to the Little Company of Mary Hospital in Evergreen Park where he was in critical condition, police said. </p><p>A man and woman escaped uninjured. </p><p>Chicago fire officials were investigating the fire. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Donate clothes to save environment, help dress Chicago-area children
entries.summary: <p>In Chicago, many families struggle to afford basic clothing, even as millions of pounds of wearable textiles are discarded each year. These parallel challenges — community need and environmental waste — are more connected than they may seem.</p><p>April, which marks both <a class="Link" href="https://www.volunteer.gov/s/article/National-Volunteer-Month" target="_blank" >National Volunteer Month</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.neefusa.org/earth-month" target="_blank" >Earth Month</a>, offers a timely opportunity to recognize that connection and act on it. Volunteer Month honors those who give their time in service of others, while Earth Month raises awareness of sustainability. Too often, we think of those as separate calls to action.</p><p>But what if one simple act could do both?</p><p>Giving back doesn't have to be complicated or costly; it can start with something as simple as cleaning out your closet and choosing to pass those items along with purpose. Each year, Americans discard <a class="Link" href="https://www.epa.gov/facts-and-figures-about-materials-waste-and-recycling/textiles-material-specific-data#:~:text=The%20Environmental%20Protection%20Agency%20(EPA)%20measures%20the,million%20tons%20*%20**Landfills**%2011.3%20million%20tons" target="_blank" >more than 17 million tons of textiles</a>, with more than two-thirds ending up in landfills, even though many of those items are still wearable.nChicago is no exception. At the same time, families across our communities are struggling to afford basic essentials like clothing for their children.</p><p>These aren't separate problems; they are two sides of the same solution.</p><p>In the right hands, an outgrown jacket or pair of shoes can make the difference between a child staying home and showing up to school prepared and confident, while also reducing waste.</p><p>That’s where volunteerism takes on a new meaning.</p><p>At <a class="Link" href="https://www.cradlestocrayons.org/chicago/" target="_blank" >Cradles to Crayons</a>, we see how volunteer-driven models can simultaneously meet basic needs for kids in the Chicago area and reduce environmental waste. Through our partnership with <a class="Link" href="https://chicagotextilerecycling.com/" target="_blank" >Chicago Textile Recycling</a>, we’ve diverted more than 1 million pounds of textiles from landfills since 2017. Proof that with the right systems in place, donated clothing can be redistributed to children or redirected through nonprofit and recycling partners to be reused, resold or repurposed.</p><p>But the impact of this work isn’t only environmental. It also brings people together. Each week, students, families, colleagues and neighbors gather around a shared purpose. At a time when loneliness and isolation are rising across the country, these moments of connection matter.</p><p><i>Dawn Melchiorre, executive director, Cradles to Crayons Chicago</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a80000" name="module-a80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Interchange fee restrictions can make life harder for many Illinoisans</h3><p>More than 4 million Illinois families rely on credit unions for safe, affordable financial services. These institutions provide essentials that support financial stability — free checking accounts, fraud protection and credit card rewards that put money back into consumers’ pockets.</p><p>Credit unions are nonprofit cooperatives owned by the people they serve. Their mission is simple: expand access to affordable financial services and strengthen local communities. Built on the principle of “people helping people,” they are often the only trusted financial lifeline for families underserved by large national banks — offering credit-building programs, affordable auto loans, and pathways to homeownership.</p><p>They also provide secure, convenient access to money through debit and credit cards. Behind every quick tap or swipe is a complex system that protects consumers from fraud. That system is supported, in part, by interchange revenue — a small fee paid by merchants when customers use electronic payments.</p><p>The Illinois Interchange Fee Prohibition Act was marketed as a c<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/other-views/2026/03/11/removing-credit-card-swipe-fees-taxes-tips-save-illinois-money-interchange-fee-prohibition-rob-karr" >onsumer protection measure</a>. In reality, it primarily benefits large national retailers while placing new strain on the community-based institutions millions of Illinoisans depend on.</p><p>Supporters claim the law will reduce costs by eliminating interchange fees on taxes and tips. But there is no requirement that merchants pass those savings onto consumers and little reason to believe they will.</p><p>We have seen this before. When Congress capped debit card interchange fees under the <a class="Link" href="https://bankingjournal.aba.com/2026/02/how-the-durbin-amendment-hurt-consumers-and-why-expanding-it-would-hurt-more/" target="_blank" >Durbin Amendment </a>in 2011, retailers promised lower prices. Instead, most kept the savings. Consumers lost free checking, saw rewards disappear and faced higher banking fees.</p><p>Illinois is now poised to repeat that mistake.</p><p>The law also creates real-world challenges. It requires payment systems to separate taxes and tips from transactions — something current infrastructure is not designed to do. Compliance will require costly upgrades to point-of-sale systems and processing networks — costs that small businesses are least able to absorb.</p><p>That leaves Illinois families stuck in the middle: fewer rewards, fewer free services and potentially higher banking costs, with little chance of lower prices. In the end, consumers could pay more — not less.</p><p>Lawmakers should repeal this flawed law before its unintended consequences make everyday banking more expensive and less accessible for the people who depend on it most.</p><p><i>Libby Calderone, president and CEO, Illinois Credit Union League</i></p><h3>Library of lies</h3><p>I trust Donald Trump's presidential library will have only one section: fiction. The material will reflect all the falsehoods and lies that have flowed from this egotistical and narcissistic excuse for a president.</p><p><i>Tony Prevolos, Bonita Springs ,Florida</i></p><h3>Speak up and change country’s trajectory</h3><p>I commend S.E. Cupp for her bold <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/09/trump-supporters-iran-bombing-truth-social-lies-racist-sexist-maga-s-e-cupp" >column</a> last week on Donald Trump supporters and how most of them are explicit examples of "compliance equals complicity." The integrity of the Republican majority is unveiled as it continually ignores the incompetence, ignorance, disregard for the law and our fraying relationship with other nations under Trump. Those who support Republican leaders need to step up and demand a move to right the administration.</p><p><i>Saul Holmes, Chatham</i></p><h3>Health tip</h3><p>As a 35-year-plus retired health care worker, I found Drs. Monica Maalouf, Winnie Lin and Alec Nicolas Angelo Jotte's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/04/04/medicare-for-all-chicago-pope-taxes-voter-rights-save-act-teen-takeovers-loyola-murder-sox-cubs" target="_blank" ><u>letter</u></a> supporting Medicare for All insightful. Realistically, I question our country's political will to struggle with the implementation issues necessitated for such a policy, I would, however, suggest a more feasible first step: universal primary and preventative care including eye, dental and hearing.</p><p><i>Tim Ressel, Glen Ellyn</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Friend's husband is quite busy behind her back
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a new close friend, and I am conflicted about whether I should inform her about her husband's misdeeds. Before we became close, I learned he had been sexting and sending nude photos to two women I know. He often makes inappropriate jokes. He recently sent me an inappropriate message, which, even if he was joking, made me uncomfortable.</p><p>My partner no longer wants to see them as a couple. It has put me in an awkward position. I love my friend and wonder if she should know about all of this. What should I do? — <i>TRUTH-TELLER IN ARKANSAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR TRUTH-TELLER:</b> Tell your new close friend everything and explain it is why you and your partner will no longer be socializing with her and her husband as a couple. If she doesn't believe what you're saying, see if she would be willing to speak with the two friends her husband has been bothering. She needs to be informed because it appears her husband has a problem that could affect her marriage.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My sister "Maggie" had brain surgery to remove a cyst a year ago. She was left with memory deficits and is obese with balance issues. Maggie has been living with her daughter for the last several months. Her favorite activity is hanging out with her granddaughters. But her daughter, pregnant with her third child, and her military family are moving in seven months, and we need to make a plan for my sister. Maggie is resistant to any discussion of this and says she'll "just go home and die." What can we do? — <i>SUPPORTIVE SIS IN THE SOUTH</i></p><p><b>DEAR SIS:</b> Your sister is going to need someplace to live after your niece and her family move. Because you don't know whether Maggie is still capable of living independently, it is important that she be psychologically and neurologically evaluated. A doctor with a specialty in neurology or geriatrics may be able to do that.</p><p>If Maggie can no longer live independently, the next step would be to research what assisted living facilities can provide her with the help she needs. If that is not feasible, a social worker may be able to assist in finding home care for your sister.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> A generous friend sent me an expensive gift, but it is something I already have. She can't easily afford these gifts. I returned it to the merchant but, unfortunately, the refund will come to me instead of her. I would like to give my friend back the money.</p><p>She doesn't need to give us anything in order to be our friend, but I worry that I'll offend her. I have asked her many times to quit giving gifts, but she continues, and they aren't gifts we can use. Her company is the only gift we need. Please advise. — <i>PRESENCE, NOT PRESENTS</i></p><p><b>DEAR P.N.P.:</b> I'll try. Recognizing that this is a sensitive subject, have a conversation with this woman and explain that you treasure her friendship, but you do not need any more "things." You have more of everything than you need. HOWEVER, if she is a baker, you would truly enjoy something from her oven. ("Nuthin' sez lovin' like ...")</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Abby shares more than 100 of her favorite recipes in two booklets: "Abby's Favorite Recipes" and "More Favorite Recipes by Dear Abby." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $16 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby, Cookbooklet Set, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: CPS students are earning biliteracy seals in record numbers. For many, it brings pride and job benefits
entries.summary: <p>When Anyeli Miranda and her family arrived in Chicago from Guatemala about seven years ago she felt like a fish out of water.</p><p>A big part of that was the language barrier she faced as a native Spanish speaker. Back home she took some English classes but knew mostly basic phrases, such as how to introduce herself. That made it challenging to connect with her classmates when she started sixth grade in Chicago.</p><p>“At first, it was a little bit difficult to adjust, to make friends, find people that will let you be part of their group,” Anyeli said.</p><p>Now a senior at Roosevelt High School in Albany Park, Anyeli thinks back on those years with pride because of how far she’s come. She’s getting ready to go to college next year to study nursing, and she's proven her skills in both Spanish and English, which she no longer sees as an obstacle.</p><p>Though it isn’t official yet, Anyeli’s graduation diploma will have a seal of biliteracy, making her one of thousands of Chicago Public Schools students who have earned a state award that recognizes students who show they can read, write and speak at a high level in English and another language.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-8c0000" name="html-embed-module-8c0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28411467"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28411467/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Last year, more than 3,500 CPS students earned the seal, a new record. That was much higher than in 2015, the first year awards were presented to graduates, when 91 students received the recognition. The total number of Illinois students who earned the seal last year also hit an all-time high.</p><p>Educators and experts say that growth is due to a combination of factors, including increased awareness of the program among families, expanded language testing and a rising interest in multilingualism, which can make students more marketable when applying for colleges or jobs.</p><p>It can also be a financial boon. Public colleges and universities in Illinois award college credit to students who have the seal, saving them time and money as they work toward their degrees. And students say the award makes them proud of themselves and their skills.</p><p>“I think it’s important to have because it’s a good opportunity that will open doors,” Anyeli said.</p><h3>Why high schools are awarding more seals of biliteracy</h3><p>In 2013, Illinois became the third state to establish a state seal of biliteracy. The first awards were presented to graduates in 2015, when around 500 students earned a seal in 10 different languages. Over the last decade, the number of recognitions has grown. Last year, nearly 11,000 students in Illinois earned the seal in 62 different languages.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b10000" name="image-b10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a62a58/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+101/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F51%2F84601e21439483fb55fbccbeb10e%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-15.jpg" alt="AP Spanish presentation at Roosevelt" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a62a58/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+101/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F51%2F84601e21439483fb55fbccbeb10e%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/488746e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+101/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F51%2F84601e21439483fb55fbccbeb10e%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a62a58/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+101/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F51%2F84601e21439483fb55fbccbeb10e%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Students listen to a presentation during an AP Spanish class at Roosevelt High School in Albany Park. Taking a high-level language class can help students prepare to earn a state seal of biliteracy.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Students who come close to meeting criteria for the seal can receive a commendation toward biliteracy, which recognizes their progress. A higher number of students are earning that commendation, too.</p><p>That reflects a growing interest in bilingualism after schools and educators spent many decades emphasizing English skills, often at the expense of students’ home languages — though the Trump administration has tried to <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/2025/08/20/trump-administration-rescinds-school-guidance-for-serving-english-learners/" target="_blank" ><u>bring back this English-only focus</u></a>.</p><p>In CPS, English learners make up a growing share of students who are earning the seal. English learners represented 13% of biliteracy seal recipients last year, up from 7% in 2024. Meanwhile, the portion of awards that went to students classified as former English learners last year dropped to 54% from 60% in 2024.</p><p>CPS graduates primarily earned the recognition in Spanish last year, but students also got seals in dozens of other languages, including Swahili, Afrikaans, Pashto and Nepali. That’s largely due to an expansion of the tests approved to evaluate students’ language proficiency.</p><p>To earn the seal, students are tested on their listening, reading, writing and speaking skills, typically on a computer. But students can also qualify for the seal by scoring high on their AP language exams, SATs or by taking certain college-level courses.</p><p>Roosevelt is one of the few high schools in CPS with a dual language program, which can help students prepare for the seal by offering rigorous coursework in two languages.</p><p>During a recent AP Spanish class, for example, Roosevelt students listened intently to their peers giving a presentation about recent cartel violence that broke out in Mexico, all in Spanish.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" alt="Natalie Cuevas stands for a portrait at her school." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a3bd82d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2Ff7%2F1694956d4048a89da8ef6ed14d94%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-24.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdcb7d4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2Ff7%2F1694956d4048a89da8ef6ed14d94%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-24.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a3bd82d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2Ff7%2F1694956d4048a89da8ef6ed14d94%2Fbiliteracyseal-260325-24.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Natalie Cuevas, the dual language coordinator at Roosevelt High School in Albany Park, reaches out to teachers and families to help raise awareness about the seal of biliteracy and how students can earn it.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Natalie Cuevas, the dual language coordinator at Roosevelt, said it takes a collaborative effort among staff to determine which students might be good candidates to take the test. Sometimes students approach her about it, but she also reaches out to families and teachers, often in world language and English classes, to let them know about the opportunity.</p><p>She’s also spread the word by making classroom presentations, putting up posters and posting on social media. That encouraged more Roosevelt students to go after the seal. Last year 42 students at Roosevelt earned the seal of biliteracy, including firsts in the state for Pashto and Afrikaans, Cuevas said. She expects more will earn the award this year.</p><h3>Students say seal instills pride and boosts job marketability</h3><p>Nicole Skorupsky, who graduated last year from Rickover Naval Academy in Portage Park, was among the first CPS students to earn a seal in Hungarian.</p><p>Skorupsky was born in the U.S., but her parents are from a region in western Ukraine where Hungarian is spoken. The couple came to the U.S. in the 1990s. Skorupsky grew up speaking Hungarian and Ukrainian at home.</p><p>American movies helped Skorupsky learn English, as well as the dual language program at her elementary school. But her parents also made sure their daughter didn’t lose her native languages. They enrolled her in a Hungarian Saturday school, and they spoke Ukrainian at home.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Nicole Skorupsky’s parents, Erika Komonyi and Yuri Skorupsky, helped her preserve her home languages by speaking Ukrainian at home and enrolling her in a Hungarian Saturday school. She was among the first CPS students to earn of seal of biliteracy in Hungarian. <br></p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo courtesy of Nicole Skorupsky</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Skorupsky, now a freshman at Loyola University Chicago, said those efforts inspired her to ask about taking the seal of biliteracy test in Hungarian when she entered high school.</p><p>“I knew that if I was to pass it, I would make my family proud because my family put so much effort into teaching me the language,” Skorupsky said.</p><p>She also thinks it'll help with finding a job because Hungarian is one of the most difficult languages for English speakers to learn, and the seal proves she took the time to become proficient.</p><p>“My future employers, my future colleges, will also see that I learned such a hard language and that I can speak it, so it'll just help me overall,” Skorupsky said.</p><p>James Cohen, president of the nonprofit Illinois Association of Multilingual Multicultural Education, said <a class="Link" href="https://www.staugustine.edu/2024/05/02/benefits-of-being-bilingual-in-the-workplace/" target="_blank" ><u>studies have shown</u></a> that people who know more than one language have increased job opportunities and often earn higher wages.</p><p>“They actually make more money if they're biliterate and bilingual,” Cohen said. “You're more marketable.”</p><p>It may also help high school graduates show they are well-rounded, which gives them a leg up when applying for colleges, Cohen said.</p><p>Cuevas said the program would have helped her in her career had she been able to earn a seal of biliteracy when she was a student.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Flags are displayed in an Advanced Placement Spanish classroom at Roosevelt High School in Albany Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“As a bilingual teacher I had to go back to school, take additional classes, take a test to prove that,” Cuevas said. The seal of biliteracy would have made it easier to show colleges and employers the skills she already possessed, she said.</p><p>Anyeli feels more capable and confident as she inches closer to officially earning her seal of biliteracy at graduation.</p><p>“It has also increased my knowledge,” she said. “I have a mind that is more open, that thinks quicker.”</p><p>Now she’s helping her parents improve their English. She said she tries to accompany her mom when she runs errands in case there’s no one around who speaks Spanish. Those trips remind her that language barriers can prevent people from getting what they need.</p><p>“For example, in hospitals sometimes people don't speak Spanish, so it can be a little complicated getting help,” Anyeli said.</p><p>That’s why she's determined to reach her goal of becoming a nurse.</p><p>“I feel very proud of myself, for the effort I put in,” Anyeli said. “I remember that 12-year-old girl who didn't know English, and I'm proud of who I am now.”</p>

entries.title: Cops did little to investigate gun's theft from station
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: The Chicago Police Department fought to keep secret the details of its investigation into a handgun taken from a room full of cops in late 2023. In the end, no one was charged, and no officers were disciplined.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement sets off echoes of Latin regimes, Black Maternal Health Week and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-y/" target="_blank" >The Bulls fell to the Mavericks</a>, 149-128; <a href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-d/" target="_blank" >Cops did little to probe gun stolen from police station and used in three shootings</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-h/" target="_blank" >Peter Nickeas</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-k/" target="_blank" >Casey Toner</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-u/" target="_blank" >Tom Schuba</a></h5><p><b>Missing gun</b>: When a valuable Glock handgun was stolen from inside a Gresham District police station in 2023, the Chicago Police Department did little to investigate, not even interviewing most of the officers who were there. That was even after the gun had been used in a series of shootings and then found near a teen caught trying to break into a car, police records show. It also was after a police supervisor told investigators he thought that another cop had taken it.</p><p><b>Case closed?</b>: Investigators closed the case without figuring out who stole the gun, which had been turned in to police and was supposed to have been destroyed. After reporters asked how a gun could have been stolen from a police station full of cops, the department said it was reopening its investigation. Even then, investigators didn’t interview a single additional officer who was there when the weapon was swiped.</p><p><b>Release refused</b>: The police department fought to keep this information secret, refusing to release case records — doing so only after the Illinois Answers Project took the city to court. In the end, no one was charged or disciplined.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>IMMIGRATION ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-b/" target="_blank" >Trump’s deportation blitz evokes painful memories of Latin American ‘disappearances’</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-n/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a></h5><p><b>One woman's story</b>: In December 1979, Neris González was leaving the market in her farm town in central El Salvador when Salvadoran national guardsmen suddenly grabbed her and took her captive. González, who was in her late teens, was taken to a prison and was brutally tortured for two weeks before finally being released. It was the beginning of the Salvadoran civil war. </p><p><b>Familiar sights</b>: More than 45 years later, González, who moved to Chicago in 1997, thought she had seen the last of militarized patrols snatching people off the streets. But then U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol agents launched Operation Midway Blitz last fall, which González said brought back painful memories. "I never imagined ... that in a country called the United States and here in Chicago, there would be such an aggressive, violent, unaccountable and military migration response," González, 70, said.</p><p><b>Past and present</b>: President Donald Trump’s mass deportation campaign has eased up of late. But in Chicago, it has left a lasting impact on the city’s Latino neighborhoods, where some residents still fear going outside. For some, the events of the last year echo a dark chapter in Central and South American history, when tens of thousands of people were detained and disappeared</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ Black Illinoisans have the highest maternal mortality rate in the state, at 78 deaths per 100,000 live births.</p><p><b>Raising awareness</b>: Saturday marked Global Black Doula Day and the start of Black Maternal Health Week, a campaign launched by Black Mamas Matter Alliance to drive activism and build community around the experiences of Black mothers, according to Ayesha Jaco, executive director of the nonprofit West Side United.</p><p><b>Vital role</b>: "A doula is a gap filler," doula Zetanefert Zipewtu said Saturday at a community baby shower held at the Garfield Park Fieldhouse to celebrate Black maternal health. "Our job is to ensure that expecting people have the information, emotional support and physical care they need to achieve their birth and postpartum goals."</p><p><a class="Link" Kenneth Anderson, pastor of Evanston’s St. John Henry Newman Parish, after church officials found Anderson used "substantial" parish funds for personal expenses.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-e/" target="_blank" ><b>Abbott Laboratories suit</b></a>: A Cook County jury has awarded $70 million to four families in their lawsuit against the company, finding Abbott’s infant formula based on cow’s milk contributed to a life-threatening condition in premature infants.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-s/" target="_blank" ><b>CHA sued</b></a>: Housing advocates filed a lawsuit Thursday contesting the Chicago Housing Authority’s decision to appoint a new CEO last month, claiming the CHA board failed to properly notify the public about the vote.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-g/" target="_blank" ><b>Philz Coffee removes Pride flags</b></a>: The decision has sparked outrage from some employees and customers, who say the LGBTQ+ community is intrinsically linked to the coffeehouse’s branding.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-w/" target="_blank" ><b>Merritt advances</b></a>: Kenya Merritt’s appointment to be Chicago’s next cultural commissioner advanced out of a City Council committee Thursday, but a whistleblower lawsuit an ex-top deputy filed against Merritt and the city dominated much of the hours-long questioning.</li></ul><hr><p> </p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-yd/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Knitting and crocheting devotees swarm YarnCon for threads and thrills</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-jd/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p></p><p>Thirty minutes after YarnCon kicked off Saturday, the fiber fair was already buzzing with chaotic energy.</p><p>Crowds were spilling into booths full of sweaters, shawls, spindles and needles. Makers were happily spinning and weaving. And people were squeezing — and sometimes smelling — yarn. Lots and lots of colorful yarn.</p><p>More than 2,000 people were expected to visit YarnCon this weekend.</p><p>The annual fair launched in 2007 to support local, independent fiber producers who couldn't afford to participate in major craft shows. After a small start at the Pulaski Fieldhouse, the event has grown to feature more than 60 vendors from Illinois and throughout the U.S.</p><p>In contrast to the frenzied environment at the fair, the act of knitting or crocheting has a calming effect for hobbyists. YarnCon attendees reported benefits to their mental health, praising the activity for helping them build community, learn a skill and find an alternative to the fast-fashion craze.</p><p>"I don’t need it," said Kimberly Carlos, of Dyer, Indiana, a self-proclaimed "fiber enthusiast" who stood in line for hours to get into the free event at Plumbers Hall in the West Loop. "Most of us don’t need it. But it’s kind of like my relaxation. Yarn is my therapy."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-jh/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> <br><b>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</b></p><p><b>How has </b><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wityhjk-l-ju/" target="_blank" ><b>Chicago’s worsening flooding</b></a><b> affected you?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3b0000" name="image-3b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1646842/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6116x3432+0+323/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F12%2Fd54694aa4e3295befa7ad441722f%2Fweather-260413-010.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e2d8f16/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6116x3432+0+323/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F12%2Fd54694aa4e3295befa7ad441722f%2Fweather-260413-010.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1d22902/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6116x3432+0+323/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F12%2Fd54694aa4e3295befa7ad441722f%2Fweather-260413-010.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicagoans enjoy warm weather by the lake near Monroe Harbor, Sunday, April 12, 2026.
