Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>En <a class="Link" href="https://www.imdb.com/title/tt16034448/" target="_blank" >“1938: Cuando México recuperó su petróleo,”</a> Sergio Bonilla y Raúl Briones protagonizan la película de Sergio Olhovich, que tardó casi dos décadas en realizarse. La historia trata sobre la decisión del presidente mexicano Lázaro Cárdenas de nacionalizar las reservas de petróleo y las instalaciones del país después de que las compañías petroleras de Estados Unidos y Gran Bretaña intentaran explotar el recurso.</p><p>Las películas animadas están fuertemente representadas este año, con la mayor cantidad proyectada en el festival.</p><p>“Soy Frankelda”, una película de fantasía oscura en stop-motion de los hermanos mexicanos <a class="Link" href="https://www.frankeldasbookofspooks.com/" target="_blank" >Arturo y Roy Ambriz</a>, está apoyada por Guillermo del Toro. En ella, los intrincados títeres, fabricados por un equipo de más de una docena de artistas, están adornados con alas plumosas, ojos coloridos y dientes afilados. Del Toro aconsejó a los hermanos y promovió ampliamente la película, diciéndoles a sus compatriotas que era un “imperdible”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div asistentes al festival serán de los primeros en Estados Unidos en verla en la pantalla grande. (Si te la pierdes, Netflix <a class="Link" href="https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/movies/movie-news/netflix-acquires-frankelda-stop-motion-animation-1236510690/" target="_blank" >adquirió los derechos de la película</a>, que estará disponible en la plataforma de streaming más adelante este año).</p><p>El tipo de creatividad de los hermanos Ambriz es exactamente el que los organizadores del festival latino buscan destacar, dijo Vargas. “Esperamos poder traer más y más [películas animadas] en las ediciones venideras”, agregó.</p><p>Y <a class="Link" href="https://www.theydreamfilm.com/" target="_blank" >“TheyDream,”</a> de William D. Caballero, es la segunda película animada en el festival de este año. La película sigue a la madre de Caballero mientras lidia con el duelo y la culpa tras la muerte de su propia madre, Isolina. La matriarca es traída de vuelta a la vida a través de animaciones 2D y 3D, técnicas documentales, fotos y videos de archivo y varias discusiones reveladoras entre madre e hijo.</p><div Cine Latino de Chicago | Proporcionada</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Más allá del español </b></h3><p>Cada película incluye subtítulos en inglés, lo que resulta especialmente útil para el puñado de películas que no están en español.</p><p>En <a class="Link" href="https://tribecafilm.com/festival/archive/runa-simi-2025" target="_blank" >“Runa Simi,”</a> un documental de Perú, el quechua ocupa un lugar central. Los cineastas siguen al artista-activista Fernando Valencia y a su hijo Dylan mientras trabajan para doblar “El Rey León” a la lengua indígena más hablada de Sudamérica.</p><p>El drama guatemalteco <a class="Link" href="https://www.imdb.com/title/tt36454397/" target="_blank" >“Cordillera de fuego”</a> presenta el kaqchikel y el tz'utujil, dos idiomas hablados por el pueblo indígena maya. Dirigida por el cineasta y guionista <a class="Link" href="https://www.imdb.com/name/nm3563342/" target="_blank" >Jayro Bustamante</a>, la película se centra en dos volcanólogos que trabajan para combatir la corrupción y salvar a familias en riesgo mientras se forma un nuevo volcán en la famosa Cordillera de Fuego de Guatemala.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Festival de Cine Latino de Chicago | Proporcionada</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Bustamante lleva otro documental guatemalteco al festival este año con <a class="Link" href="https://www.comparsafilm.com/" target="_blank" >“Comparsa.”</a> Después de que 41 niñas fueron asesinadas en una casa segura dirigida por el gobierno, las hermanas Lesli y Lupe Pérez en Ciudad Peronia utilizan el arte para sanar profundas heridas en la comunidad. Han dado vida a un creciente movimiento juvenil que busca recuperar la seguridad y el espacio para las mujeres y niñas guatemaltecas.</p><p>El criollo haitiano y el francés se hablan en <a class="Link" href="https://aguijontheater.org/home-back/about-us/ensemble/rey-andujar/" target="_blank" >“Melodrama”</a>, un drama escrito por el novelista <a class="Link" href="https://aguijontheater.org/home-back/about-us/ensemble/rey-andujar/" target="_blank" >Rey Andújar</a> y el director dominicano <a class="Link" href="https://www.berlinale-talents.de/bt/talent/andresenrique-fariascintron/profile" target="_blank" >Andrés Farías</a>. La película cuenta la historia de Sonia, quien conoce y se enamora de Aimé, un obrero de construcción haitiano, justo cuando el gobierno de la República Dominicana comienza su represión contra los haitianos indocumentados.</p><p>El portugués aparece en al menos una media docena de películas, desde “Five Types of Fear” de Bruno Brini hasta “A Wolf among the Swans”, un biopic sobre Thiago Soares, el principal bailarín del Royal Ballet de Londres hasta que se retiró hace seis años.</p><p>El idioma también se habla en <a class="Link" href="https://www.7thart.com/product/a-shabbat-on-the-other-side-of-the-river/" target="_blank" >“A Shabbat on the Other Side of the River”</a>, del director Diego Lajst. La película es un documental sobre la comunidad judía marroquí que ha llamado hogar a la vasta selva amazónica durante dos siglos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c5dc40" name="image-c5dc40"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" año.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Festival de Cine Latino de Chicago | Proporcionada</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Estrenándose junto a la película de Lajst está el cortometraje <a class="Link" href="https://www.futureoffilmisfemale.com/barrio-chino-havana" target="_blank" >“Barrio Chino Havana,”</a> que cuenta la historia de la comunidad afro-cubano-china en Cuba. Miles de trabajadores chinos viajaron en barco al Caribe a mediados del siglo XIX y formaron grandes comunidades como la de la película. El barrio chino de Cuba está comenzando a desvanecerse, pero se estima que alrededor de 1 de cada 6 cubanos tiene ascendencia china.</p><h3><b>Hecho en Chicago </b></h3><p>Sólo cuatro producciones realizadas en Chicago están incluidas en la programación del festival de este año.</p><p>Vargas y Bernal dijeron que la falta de películas de Chicago este año refleja los desafíos que enfrentan los cineastas independientes. La producción de programas como “The Bear” y la franquicia One Chicago (“Chicago P.D.”, “Chicago Med” y “Chicago Fire”) está en auge, pero esos proyectos cuentan con financiamiento y conexiones detrás de escena.</p><p>El cineasta y profesor de DePaul <a class="Link" href="https://www.bzahm.com/" target="_blank" >Brian Zahm</a> presentó <a class="Link" href="https://www.bzahm.com/abel" target="_blank" >“Abel,”</a> que cuenta la historia de vida de Abel Berumen, un fotógrafo local cuya vida cambió después de que un día despertó ciego. “Abel” se estrenará junto a <a class="Link" href="https://latidofilms.com/films/deaf/" target="_blank" >“Sorda”</a>, una película inspirada en la hermana de la directora española Eva Libertad.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Council committee backs raising Chicago cab fares by 20% to save ailing taxi industry
entries.summary: <p>Chicago taxicab fares <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fran-spielman-show/2026/02/12/chicago-taxicab-industry-fare-hike-proposal-business-affairs-commissioner-ivan-capifali" >could soon rise</a> by 20% — the first rate hike in a decade — to save a once-dominant industry whose monopoly was, as one City Council member put it, “eviscerated” by Uber and Lyft.</p><p>Downtown Ald. Brendan Reilly (42nd) cast the only dissenting vote as the measure won backing from the Committee on License and Consumer Protection Monday, even after he acknowledged that the city’s failure to raise cab fare rates has made it “increasingly difficult for drivers to maintain their livelihood and meet regulatory standards” imposed by the city.</p><p>“As someone who tries to support the taxi industry in the wake of Uber and Lyft, more often than not when I enter into a taxicab — a medallioned vehicle — I have a driver refusing to pull the flag,” Reilly said. “It is after I identify myself and what I do that driver — sometimes they’ll agree to pull the flag So, I wonder for the average citizen of Chicago what their experience might be.”</p><p>Business Affairs and Consumer Protection Commissioner Ivan Capifali told Reilly, “ The same thing has happened to me in my personal life. It is something that this ordinance is looking to address” with a fine that would increase tenfold — from $100 to $1,000 for failing to start the meter.</p><p>Reilly was not assuaged after Capifali acknowledged under questioning that BACP has only five investigators assigned to police 3,000 taxicabs, and was unable to tell Reilly how many tickets those investigators have written to punish drivers who fail to pull the flag when a passenger enters. </p><p>“To be clear, I’m sympathetic to the taxicab industry because they’ve been eviscerated by Uber and Lyft. I certainly don’t want to do anything more to hurt that industry than the city already had. I am sympathetic to the need to adjust rates so the drivers can make a living,” Reilly said.</p><p>“However, when we see rampant abuse and flouting of the law, it makes it harder for me to extend that sympathy towards voting for a substantial fare increase.”</p><p>The fare hike poised for full Council approval on Wednesday was introduced by Mayor Brandon Johnson last fall while public attention was focused on the battle over his 2026 budget.</p><p>It has languished in committee ever since — until Monday when Capifali told the License Committee that the first fare hike in a decade was desperately needed to accommodate the “high cost of everything,” from insurance, vehicle maintenance and replacement to skyrocketing fuel prices.</p><p>The mayor’s plan would maintain the cost of entering a cab, known as the “flag pull,” at $3.25 for the first one-ninth of a mile, but provide substantial relief for cab drivers in other ways.</p><p>That includes a $2.50 rush hour fee for rides between 3:30 p.m. and 7 p.m., a $1 overnight fee for rides between 8 p.m. and 6 a.m. and increased charges for both time and distance.</p><p>Instead of 25 cents for each additional one-ninth mile, passengers would pay 31 cents. Another 31 cents would be tacked on to the fare for every 45 seconds instead of 20 cents for every 36 seconds.</p><p>Shared rides for “a minimum of two passengers” would also rise — from $24 to $30 from O’Hare Airport to downtown or McCormick Place — and from $18 to $22 from Midway to downtown or the convention center. The cost of shared rides between Midway and O’Hare would rise from $37 to $45.</p><p>The cost of cleaning up a cab after an inebriated or unruly rider would double — from $50 to $100.</p><p>“I appreciate the vomit fee doubling. I don’t think you can clean anything for fifty bucks. But I do think we need a new name,” said North Side Ald. Bennett Lawson (44th).</p><p>Lawson said the $1 fuel surcharge imposed by former Mayor Lori Lightfoot whenever gasoline prices rise above $5 a gallon for one month would remain in effect under Johnson’s plan.</p><p>“We’re getting there. Five dollars a gallon is not far off if the trend continues. I kind of want to put a point on that for my colleagues and others. On top of this, we’re going to get a fuel surcharge soon,” Lawson said.</p><p>The License Committee also authorized BACP to issue "provisional city licenses" to 253 restaurants, bars, and bowling alleys with an ancillary liquor license to install <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/12/video-gambling-battle-chicago-city-council-brandon-johnson-license-committee-rejection" >video gambling terminals</a> while waiting for the Illinois Gaming Board to approve their permanent VGT applications.</p><p>The alternate budget approved by the City Council majority that rejected Johnson's corporate head tax assumed that the city would generate $6.8 million in licensing revenue, but the Johnson administration remains opposed to the plan and hasn't moved on it. </p><p>"We passed VGT. It's in the budget. We need to start to get some revenue from it as soon as possible or we're going to have more budget problems than we already have," Lawson said.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Aurora native Noah Schultz brings hometown edge to White Sox debut
entries.summary: <p>Noah Schultz, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ top pitching prospect, hasn’t come all that far from the family Wiffle ball games he played growing up in Aurora.</p><p>Only 45 miles or so, in fact, by the time he towers over the mound at Rate Field for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/12/white-sox-call-up-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci" target="_blank" ><u>his major-league debut</u></a> Tuesday, carrying a good chunk of the rebuilding Sox’ hopes with his left arm.</p><p>And while the limbs on Schultz’s staggering 6-10 frame have grown longer, he’s still the same fiercely competitive kid who never backed down from a backyard contest with older sisters Ashley and Emily, according to their mother, Kim Schultz.</p><p>‘‘Very competitive family,’’ she said on the eve of her son’s first big-league start. ‘‘The Wiffle ball in the backyard, the arguing — really, the competition just was always high.’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-right> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cb0000" name="image-cb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium-3x4"> <source type="image/webp" width="375" height="500" height="500" alt="Noah Schultz, pictured at age 9." width="375" height="500" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c33b5f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1262x1683+0+0/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2F69%2Fb474d1194f1fa1e8a088dc0b0f2b%2Fimg-7444.JPEG" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Noah Schultz, pictured at age 9.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>You might not get that impression from the soft-spoken Schultz right away — at least once you get over his imposing stature. But his mom knows it’s what first made him a star at Oswego East High School, then a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/7/17/23267956/white-sox-select-oswego-east-pitcher-noah-schultz-with-with-first-pick-in-draft" target="_blank" >first-round draft pick</a> for the Sox in 2022 and now one of the most-anticipated arrivals in baseball, rated No. 46 by MLB Pipeline.</p><p>‘‘He’s just a different person out there, and that’s what needs to happen: He’s got to focus,’’ Kim Schultz said. ‘‘He’s probably just really, really excited and probably jumping out of his skin, but he’s got a really good knack of really focusing when he gets out there and gets the ball.’’</p><p></p><p>That wasn’t a concern when Schultz found out Saturday he had been called up from Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Super-excited and just pretty much screaming,’’ Kim Schultz said of her son, who grew up a Dodgers fan, thanks to his dad, Larry. ‘‘Literally a 15-second phone call, and he was on a plane the next day.</p><p></p><p>‘‘He’s a pretty quiet kid, shy kid. But I see the spurts of the 22-year-old. He’s still young and does kid stuff. But he’s a big kid.’’</p><p></p><p>Just ask the 248 hitters Schultz struck out in the minors en route to the Sox, usually with a four-seam fastball that tops out at 99 mph or a devastating slider that invites comparisons to that of Hall of Famer Randy Johnson — with whom Schultz will be tied as second-tallest player to suit up for a major-league squad.</p><p></p><p>‘‘He’s an electric guy; he’s an electric pitcher,’’ said Sox shortstop Colson Montgomery, who played with Schultz at Charlotte and knows a thing or two about shouldering big expectations. ‘‘We’ve all seen it for so long. We are all ready for it. We know he’s a true competitor.’’</p><p></p><p>Manager Will Venable said that while the Sox are monitoring Schultz’s innings, their potential ace of the future isn’t just coming up for a big-league cup of coffee after striking out 19 hitters in 14 Triple-A innings this spring.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-credit"><p>John Antonoff/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>‘‘The plan is not to bring him up and send him back out [to Charlotte],’’ Venable said. ‘‘The thought is that we bring him up here when he’s ready and go and let him do his thing. He’s about as nice a guy and polite a guy as can be, but he does have an edge when he goes out there.’’</p><p>Schultz will have an extra home-field advantage for his first outing, with some 250 supporters in attendance, including the entire Oswego East baseball team and the big sisters who schooled him in the backyard. (Emily played softball at Stanford, Ashley at Northwestern.)</p><p></p><p>‘‘We’ve had a lot of support along the way, and you have to have that — supportive parents, sisters, family, uncles, aunts, trainers, high school, everyone,’’ Kim Schultz said. ‘‘Everyone really kept their eye out for him, which was so helpful. This is a team effort, for sure.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I’d say I’m nervous for him, but I’m really not. I’m like: ‘You did it. You got it. You got where you wanted to go.’ I’m proud.’’</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a
